title: International Diversification Is Finally Paying Off
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: International diversification has historically shown long-term benefits, often underperforming in the short term but outperforming in the long term. Recent tren

content.introduction: International diversification has historically shown long-term benefits, often underperforming in the short term but outperforming in the long term. Recent trends suggest international stocks are now more attractive than U.S. shares. This shift may indicate that diversification is finally delivering expected returns.
body: A Wealth of Common Sense. International diversification has been an anchor around the necks of balanced investors for some time now:. Outperformance between America and the rest of the world has historically been cyclical. Here’s what I wrote back in December:. *In 20 years of managing money I have never witnessed more dismal sentiment for international stocks, value stocks and really valuations in general.*. I know you could have said the same thing the past 5-7 years or so but it feels like the dam truly broke this year. Here’s the thing — international stocks have been cheaper than U.S. shares for some time now. So the big question has always been: What’s the catalyst? This is a huge divergence from the austerity measures implemented by Germany in the 2010s, one of the big reasons the EU has lagged so badly behind the U.S. economy. Maybe all of the onerous rules and regulations and lack of innovation in Europe will make it hard to sustain the outperformance going forward.
have shifted the playing field—international equities, gold, silver, and uranium are now driving real portfolio performance. If you've been

In the context of global investing and under some very basic assumptions, diversification implies that a portfo-lio of global equity markets should produce a risk-adjusted return superior to that of any one country held in isolation. We explored this hypothesis by decomposing returns into (1) a component arising from multiple expan-sion (or contraction) and (2) a component arising from economic performance.4 By investigating the dynamics of these return contributors, we attempted to offer additional insight into why global diversification can disappoint over the short term but be the free (and hearty!) lunch that theory and common sense say it should be over the long term.5 Data We analyzed diversification benefits from the per-spectives of local investors in the following 22 countries for January 1950–December 2008: Austra-lia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hong Kong, Ireland, Italy, Japan, the Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, and the United States.
